text: Pet Parenting Style Questionnaire

This questionnaire is designed to help you better understand your typical style of interacting with your pet. There are 36 statements in total. Please answer as honestly as possible and use the full range of the scale when needed. Your result will be shown only to you. At the end, we will ask separately whether you would like to share your answers for research purposes.
For each statement, choose how often this behavior is true for you in your everyday life with your pet.
There are no 'good' or 'bad' answers. The goal is to reflect your usual style as accurately as possible.
Most people complete this questionnaire in about 5–10 minutes.

More clearly expressed style

Your parenting style: Authoritative.\n\nThis style typically reflects a combination of warmth, attention to the pet's emotional state, and fairly consistent guidance. Caregivers with this style tend to consider their pet's feelings and needs, spend time with their pet, show patience, and at the same time maintain structure in everyday interactions. Pets of authoritative caregivers are more often described as securely attached, highly sociable, more successful in problem-solving tasks, and more resilient to stress. Overall, this style is generally considered the most balanced.

Your parenting style: Authoritarian.\n\nThis style reflects a higher level of control, directiveness, and expectation of obedience. Caregivers with this style are more likely to set strict rules and insist that they be followed. One strength of this approach may be clarity and structure. However, in research, authoritarian parenting has been associated with higher stress in pets, less secure attachment, and poorer learning outcomes compared with the authoritative style. Paying more attention to your pet's emotional state and using a more flexible approach, for example scolding less, relying less on leash tension or other restrictive methods, and noticing and reinforcing desirable behavior more often, may improve your pet's welfare, learning, and your relationship with your pet.

Your parenting style: Permissive.\n\nThis style typically reflects a warm and caring attitude toward the pet, but with comparatively less consistency and structure. Caregivers with this style often try to avoid being harsh and may feel unsure about how best to respond to unwanted behavior. Clearer boundaries and greater predictability, for example, making sure that 'no' always means 'no,' 'yes' always means 'yes,' and keeping routines more consistent, could improve your pet's learning, strengthen your attachment, and help reduce vulnerability to stress.

a higher level of demandingness and lower emotional responsiveness to the pet's everyday experience. In research, authoritarian parenting has been associated with higher stress in pets, less secure attachment, and poorer learning outcomes compared with the authoritative style. Boundaries are important, but more warmth, attention, and gentleness in the relationship may benefit both you and your pet.

lower predictability from the pet's point of view, which may make learning and the quality of attachment more difficult. It may be helpful to pay closer attention to consistency in your own actions and to setting clear, understandable boundaries.

a combination of warmth, attention to the pet's emotional state, and consistent guidance. Pets of authoritative caregivers are more often described as securely attached, highly sociable, more successful in problem-solving tasks, and more resilient to stress.
